dialogue.utterance: Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having shortness of breath for about 15 minutes. It's worse than usual, and my nebulizer didn't help.
Are you alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event?
Yes, I know who I am, this is my living room in [CITY], it's 2019, and you're my EMTs.
What day is it and where are we headed next?
It's Tuesday, and we're going to the hospital.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event. We'll document this as AOX4.
What is your name, and did you call 911 yourself?
My name is Linda, and yes, I called 911 because I couldn't catch my breath.
Your airway is patent, and you don't appear to have any obstruction. You're breathing without assistance.
You're tachypneic, with increased work of breathing. Let me take a closer look at your breathing.
I notice accessory muscle use and you have an increased respiratory effort, with prolonged expirations.
Your radial pulses are strong, equal, and regular on both sides. Skin is warm, dry, and has normal coloration.
You mentioned COPD. How was this episode different from the usual one?
I've had COPD for years and use my nebulizer two to three times daily. This one started suddenly and didn't get better. I've also had chest congestion and coughed up dark red sputum for a week now.
Just to confirm-your name is Linda and you are a 60-year-old female with no known allergies?
That's correct. I've also had primary hypertension and need renal dialysis.
Do you have pain of any kind?
No, I don't have any pain at this time.
Heart rate is 95. Radial pulses are strong and equal bilaterally.
Respiratory rate is 28, and I note increased work of breathing.
Blood pressure is 137 over 95 mmHg.
Glucose is 116 mg/dL.
Oxygen saturation is 89%. I will start you on 3 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula.
EKG shows sinus rhythm at this time.
Your skin is warm, dry, with normal color. Capillary refill is less than 2 seconds.
Your head is normocephalic with no signs of trauma. Pupils are round, equal, reactive to light, and 3mm. No jugular venous distention noted.
You also have an AV fistula on your left upper arm with a good thrill.
Mental status: you are alert and oriented. GCS is 15. Pupils are tracking normally without abnormalities.
Your chest has normal rise and fall. There's expiratory wheezing bilaterally in upper lung fields with diminished sounds in the lower fields.
No rales, rhonchi, or asymmetry in chest movement noted. No signs of chest wall tenderness.
Auscultation reveals no stridor, just expiratory wheezing in the upper lobes.
Your abdomen is soft and non-tender. I do not note any distension or masses.
Your gait is normal. No unilateral weakness noted.
You have movement in all extremities. No signs of edema or angulation.
I'll give you a nebulized treatment with 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Ipratropium, in a combination we call Duoneb, for bronchodilation.
Then you'll receive a single dose of 10 mg Dexamethasone IV to help reduce airway inflammation.
We'll start an intravenous line in your right antecubital fossa with an 18-gauge cannula.
I'll apply a 4-lead ECG monitor to track heart rhythm during treatment.
We'll attach capnography to monitor your end-tidal CO₂ levels.
A 12-lead ECG is now being placed to provide a full cardiac assessment.
Let me check the EKG again to ensure no pathologies are missed before transport.
Updated vitals: 135/95 mmHg, 86 bpm, 29 respirations per minute. SpO2 is 100%, and EKG still shows sinus rhythm.
Another round of Duoneb is being started now; it should provide additional relief.
To support your breathing, I'll place you in semi-Fowler's position. This will help increase your lung expansion.
You're stable enough for transport. We will be taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] in [CITY] for further care.
Latest vitals: 127/86 mmHg, 90 bpm, 21 respirations per minute. SpO2 is 97%. EKG still shows sinus rhythm.
A second round of Albuterol and Ipratropium is in progress now while we're en route to the hospital.
You are now secured safely on the stretcher. We're ready to move for transport. Please remain calm.
We are now en route to [COMPANY_NAME]. It's about a 10-minute ride.
Let me double-check your vitals in transit. Everything remains stable.
Second DuoNeb dose is still running. You may begin to feel some relief.
Base, we're on scene and en route with a stable 60-year-old female with COPD exacerbation. Vital signs are stable.
All interventions are in place. Patient is secure and comfortable.
Arrival at [COMPANY_NAME]. Let me inform the ED team of your status and treatments performed.
ED team, we have a 60-year-old female with COPD exacerbation, treated with DuoNeb and Dexamethasone. IV and O2 secured. She is alert and stable.
Linda, we are done with our interventions and are handing you over to the hospital staff. They will now manage your care.
